title: Word Sums, Matrices, and Suffixing Conventions
subtitle: The Tools of Structured Word Inquiry
summary: Provides hands-on practice with the core representational tools of SWI: word sums, matrices, and suffixing conventions. Teachers learn to write and verify word sums, build and read matrices, and apply the four join conventions (Replace, Double, Change, Toggle) using the 'Check the Joins!' framework.
learning_outcomes: Write accurate word sums using standard notation (base + affix → word)
Verify word sums by testing for shared sense across a word family
Build and read matrices as generative maps of word families
Apply the four suffixing conventions: Replace, Double, Change, Toggle
Use the 'Check the Joins!' framework to analyze what happens when morphemes meet
Identify when <-es> is an orthographic outcome of <-s>, not a separate suffix
key_concepts: Suffixing convention: Replace (final non-syllabic <e> replaced by vowel suffix)
Suffixing convention: Double (final consonant doubled before vowel suffix)
Suffixing convention: Change (letter changes per orthographic convention)
Suffixing convention: Toggle (predictable swap tied to convention)
Shared sense as the test for word family membership
swi_connections: Word sums make Question 2 (How is it built?) visible and testable
Matrices operationalize Question 3 (What other words are related?)
Join conventions connect structure to spelling at the morpheme boundary
All tools connect back to meaning as the anchor
int_connections: Word sums and matrices make structure visible, reducing hidden processing
Named conventions (Replace, Double, Change, Toggle) replace vague 'rules'
Predictable tools support executive function and working memory
Explicit join language avoids confusing framings like 'drop the e'
